ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chocolate-coated digestive biscuit; closest defensible parent class is biscuit food product (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits and crackers — chocolate-coated wholemeal biscuit approximated under FoodEx2 composite biscuit category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for chocolate digestive biscuit; A000E (biscuits and crackers) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chocolate digestive biscuit as a food concept.

main_ingredients: milk chocolate or dark chocolate coating

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is 2 chocolate digestive biscuits, approximately 30 g, providing around 146 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Digestive Biscuit (Chocolate) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Digestive Biscuit (Chocolate), CR ID F11080021. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for chocolate digestive biscuits (USDA SR Legacy #18165) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; value cross-checked against McVitie's product label data for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for digestive biscuit
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 40 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain digestive biscuit (wholemeal wheat base). The chocolate coating is not expected to substantially raise GI given the fat-slowing effect on gastric emptying; this value is treated as an analogous estimate for the chocolate-coated variant. GL per serving calculated as: GI (40) × carbohydrate per serving (18.6 g per 30 g serving) / 100 = 7.4. Input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.466; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved source.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (26.81 g/100 g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention; moderate sodium (278 mg/100 g); high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit chocolate digestive biscuits due to very high sugar (26.81 g/100 g), which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises blood pressure independently of dietary sodium, compounded by high saturated fat that contributes to arterial stiffness; limit to no more than 1 biscuit occasionally.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 26.81 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; total carbohydrate 62 g/100 g with only 3.46 g/100 g dietary fibre provides insufficient buffering against post-prandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chocolate digestive biscuits are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the very high sugar content (26.81 g/100 g) will cause significant post-meal blood glucose elevation, and the high total carbohydrate load (62 g/100 g) with low fibre (3.46 g/100 g) provides inadequate glycaemic buffering; these biscuits should be avoided or replaced with lower-sugar, higher-fibre alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g from palm oil and chocolate coating) raising LDL cholesterol; trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from palm oil processing; very high sugar (26.81 g/100 g) promoting triglyceride elevation and endothelial dysfunction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid chocolate digestive biscuits due to high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g) that raises LDL cholesterol, the presence of trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) which is atherogenic at any dose, and very high sugar (26.81 g/100 g) that elevates triglycerides and promotes endothelial dysfunction; occasional consumption of no more than 1 biscuit is the maximum advisable intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) triggers RA hard gate; high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g) and sugar (26.81 g/100 g) are strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chocolate digestive biscuits are unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; trans fat from palm oil processing is pro-inflammatory at any amount, and the combination of high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g) and very high sugar (26.81 g/100 g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity, worsening joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate digestive biscuits are a popular British-origin snack biscuit consisting of a semi-sweet wholemeal wheat base coated on one side with milk or dark chocolate. The biscuit base is made from wholemeal wheat flour, oatmeal, sugar, vegetable fat (typically palm oil), and raising agents, giving it a slightly crumbly, grainy texture with a mild wheaty flavour. The chocolate coating adds sweetness, richness, and a smooth contrast to the coarser biscuit base. Per 100 g, the biscuit provides 488 kcal, 6.22 g protein, 24.06 g fat (of which approximately 11.5 g is saturated fat from palm oil and cocoa butter), 62 g carbohydrate, 26.81 g sugar, 3.46 g dietary fibre, and 278 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 40 (low GI), but the glycemic load per standard 30 g serving is 7.4, and the very high sugar content (26.81 g/100 g) makes this biscuit clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The presence of trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g from palm oil processing) also makes it unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Despite containing wholemeal flour, the dominant nutritional profile is high sugar, high saturated fat, and high total carbohydrate, limiting its health value. Chocolate digestive biscuits are widely consumed as a snack or with tea and are sold in Singapore supermarkets under brands such as McVitie's. They are not a traditional Singaporean dish but are a common imported snack in the local food environment.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080021, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest match and cross-checked against product label data. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for plain digestive biscuit as analogous estimate for the chocolate-coated variant.
notes: 1. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this product; these are confirmed zero values as reported by the approved source, not missing data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields not listed above are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrient data for this specific chocolate digestive biscuit product are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of April 2026. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source for this product. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g is an estimate based on palm oil processing characteristics documented in USDA FoodData Central for comparable chocolate-coated biscuit products; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source. 5. saturated_fat_g of 11.5 g/100 g is estimated from USDA SR Legacy entry for chocolate digestive biscuits and cross-checked against McVitie's product label for plausibility; HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this item. 6. GI value of 40 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain digestive biscuit (wholemeal wheat base) as an analogous estimate; the input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.466, which is not an approved source and has been replaced. 7. This food is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null. 8. Halal status is marked false as standard McVitie's chocolate digestive biscuits are not certified halal in Singapore; consumers should verify product-specific halal certification.
